text.title: Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters) Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every arch-pattern-hexagonal application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: The application core MUST depend only on port interfaces, never on adapter implementations. All inbound ports (use-case interfaces) and outbound ports (repository, service interfaces) MUST live inside core/ports/, NOT inside the adapters directory.
rule.rationale: If the core imports an adapter directly, swapping the adapter requires modifying the core — this breaks the pattern's fundamental guarantee. Port interfaces decouple the hexagon from its surroundings.

rule.statement: Core services MUST be tested with in-memory adapter implementations of all outbound ports, never against real databases or HTTP services. This keeps the core test suite fast (<1s) and environment-independent.
rule.rationale: If core service tests require a database, every developer needs a local DB, CI needs it, and tests become flaky on network timeouts. In-memory adapters eliminate the dependency entirely.

rule.statement: Define an inbound port per use-case family and an outbound port per external concern. Adapters implement ports; ports never import adapters.
rule.rationale: Without per-direction ports, the application core leaks runtime concerns and loses testability.

rule.statement: Driven adapters MUST translate third-party DTOs/types into domain types at the adapter boundary; the port signature MUST NOT expose external SDK types.
rule.rationale: Without an ACL, third-party API changes ripple into the domain and force a rewrite.

rule.statement: An import-direction lint MUST run in CI and block domain.* imports of adapters.* or framework code.
rule.rationale: Without lint enforcement the pattern decays in 1-2 quarters.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
